data.title: Hutchison Port Holdings Trust's Improved Results In 2024 Did Not Result In A Higher DPU
data.description:
Investment thesis

It has been two years since we covered Hutchison Port Holdings Trust (OTCPK:HUPHY) (OTCPK:HCTPF).

At that time, we were too optimistic in our view as we upgraded this penny stock from a Hold to a Buy. To get some more general information of this business trust, please read our previous article.

Market capitalization is now US$ 2.23 billion.

As usual, we start by going through the company’s latest financial results, followed by an assessment of the business environment going forward.

2024 FY Financial Results

In 2024, the Trust handled 22.3 million TEUs through its container terminals in Hong Kong and Shenzhen, mainland China.

Their terminals in Hong Kong handled 7.1 million TEUs, and the terminals just across the border in Shenzhen handled twice as much at 15.2 million.

It is interesting to see the reduction over the years in Hong Kong and the growth in Shenzhen.

The volume of TEUs handled increased by 5% year-on-year. With this volume growth, the revenue also increased year-on-year from HK$10.64 billion to HK$11.57 billion.

Their cost of finance reduced from HK$872 million to HK$855 million.

Total comprehensive income in 2024 was HK$1.99 billion, up from HK$1.19 billion the previous year. However, only HK$512 million of that comprehensive income is attributable to unitholders. The other HK$1.47 billion goes to non-controlling interests.

Nevertheless, the HK$512 million profit for the year 2024 is a lot better than the loss of HK$1.9 million it chalked up in 2023.

The main reason the finance cost was reduced is that they reduced their bank debt last year from HK$20.87 billion to HK$20.29 billion. Cash is stable at HK$8.14 billion, so their net debt is HK$12.15 billion.

Cash flow improved, as the cash from operations was HK$4.29 billion. An improvement of 13.7% year-on-year.

Now, let us look at the distribution to unitholders since the inception of this business trust.

Source

It is a far cry from the HK cents 51.24 per unit that it distributed in 2012. We are disappointed that unitholders did not get a higher DPU in 2024 than what we got in 2023.

The present yield is just over 11%, which is a good dividend yield. It is also safely covered by the free cash flow.

The business environment

The most used word in the press these days has to be “tariffs”. Every day, as we turn on the news, whether radio or television, each story starts with what POTUS has done in the last 24 hours.

Trade without any form of taxation or tariffs is, for now, a thing of the past.

Since the bulk of the damages is steered towards China, how this is going to play out is important to HCTPF. Almost all their business is based on the import and export of containerized cargoes. There are some domestic shipments, but in the greater scheme of things, this is small.

Therefore, a reduction in trade between the U.S. and EU with China will be negative for HCTPF.

How big will the reduction be?

The World Trade Organization has recently attempted to put a number on how much reduction there will be to global trade. In their report of 16th April, they estimate that present tariffs, including proposed reciprocal tariffs, would lead to a 1.5% decline in world merchandise trade in 2025. Each country will see a different outcome. Some may even gain if they are particularly favourably traded compared to the rest, and if they can supply what is required.

For China, their report states that:

Chinese merchandise exports are projected to rise by 4% to 9% across all regions outside North America, as trade is redirected. At the same time, US imports from China are expected to fall sharply in sectors such as textiles, apparel, and electrical equipment, creating new export opportunities for other suppliers able to fill the gap.”

It remains to be seen if other countries can “pick up the slack” from what the U.S. has imported over the last years. We see that President Xi is doing the rounds in South East Asia visiting their leaders to try to drum up some new business.

The WTO report also stated that it believes the U.S. will see a 10% reduction in imports. Let us just assume that China will also export 10% less than what it did in 2024.

It would not be catastrophic for HCTPF.

Furthermore, we believe these shocks are not permanent. This, too, will change, as they say.

Risk to thesis

A full-blown recession is the highest risk in our opinion. In such an environment, there is nowhere to hide.

The probability of this has risen lately, with the U.S. investment bank Goldman Sachs (GS) setting the probability at 45% and JPMorgan Chase & Co. (JPM) setting it at 60%.

We do a lot of risk assessments in our daily work, and such a high probability is not something we take lightly.

However, we believe that everything is now very fluid.

Which is also why we as investors are for the most holding on to our cash and not making any purchases at this moment. This brings us to our conclusion.

Conclusion

We all learn lessons throughout our lives. We learned one, for sure.

When the richest man in Asia wants to sell you something, it is probably not a good time to buy it. The 85% drop in the unit price since the IPO is a testament to that.

It is said that IPO stands for It’s Probably Overpriced. For HCTPF, that was the case.

Our patience is starting to run thin, as we are not convinced that management's interests are well aligned with those of the shareholders.

The fact that improved earnings and cash flow did not result in a higher DPU tells us that shareholders' interests are not at the top of their list of priorities.

The share’s valuation is not bad. The P/E on a TTM basis is 14.7, and the P/B is attractive at 0.381.

As we get paid more than 11% to wait for better days to come, these fundamentals are the reason we are holding it and not selling it at this moment.

However, we downgrade from the previous Buy to that of a Hold.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Anatomy Of A U.S. Treasury Sell-Off
data.description:
By Scott DiMaggio, CFA

Transcript

Scott DiMaggio - The bond market has been extremely volatile the past couple of weeks since the introduction of global tariffs by the US. Bond yields have sold off almost 50 basis points, and today we'd like to examine why did that occur, what's next, and how should investors think about duration in this environment?

We think there are four key factors that have contributed to the US Treasury sell-off in April. The first reason is volatility in the equity markets. The volatility in the equity markets has led to deleveraging and has led to margin calls, which has caused forced sales of US Treasuries.

These Treasuries wind up on dealer balance sheets. We see repo rates inch higher as a way to reflect that lack of buyers in the market. We do not think the unwind of the basis trade contributed to the sell-off, but it certainly was a factor once momentum had begun.

Reason number two is tariffs. We don't know quite the magnitude or the extent of the tariffs, but we do know that tariffs will contribute to inflation in 2025. And this is making investors nervous. We also know the Federal Reserve has commented that they are worried about tariffs and the inflationary impact, and that they might be hesitant to reduce interest rates.

Factor number three is the fiscal concerns. They're intensifying as the budget-reconciliation blueprint progresses through Congress. Rumored additional deficit worries are spooking the bond market and investors and are causing additional pressure on the term premium, especially out to 30 years.

Factor four, foreign investor divestment. Secretary Bessent recently confirmed that there was no evidence of foreign selling of US Treasuries in April. We're going to put this more in the rumor right now more so than the fact category, but it is something to keep an eye on as we roll through this global tariff war.

This is no time to panic on duration. We have low visibility into what tariff rates will be, what countries they will affect, and what industries will be impacted.

What we do believe is that all of this uncertainty — and tariffs themselves — are going to cause a dramatic slowing in US economic growth. This dramatic slowdown will impact the jobs market, and eventually the Federal Reserve will be forced to cut interest rates, we think three to four times this year. This monetary easing should be supportive of the bond market, especially for bonds maturing in less than 10 years.

It's imperative that we closely monitor the US fiscal situation. The reconciliation bill is making its way through Congress. We have insight that it should be completed by the beginning of June. We do expect the budget deficit to be around 6%, which, we think, is well in line with market expectations and should be supportive for the bond market.

We expect the upcoming auction of Treasury bonds to be well received by both US and international investors. Clients should expect the Fed to support the bond market if there are signs of distress. So far, we have seen periods of some lower liquidity where bid-asks have widened, but overall, no systemic problems, no real stress in the Treasury market. We believe there is zero probability of a US Treasury default centered around the debt-ceiling debate.

Given this outlook, we're focused on three ways to manage duration risk.

Stay active. We strongly believe that Treasuries will exhibit negative correlations for when risk assets come under duress. Volatility remains high, which will create opportunities to both buy and sell. Currently, with yields around 4.5%, we think this is a good entry point for duration.

We maintain our belief that the US economy will decelerate and that will cause the Federal Reserve to reduce interest rates. This tends to be a good environment for curve steepeners, and investors should consider adding positions at the front end of the curve.

One silver lining is that Treasury Secretary Scott Bessent is keenly focused on funding costs and term premium in the bond market. While structural pressures are building, this level of attention by policymakers should act as a potential buffer and helps to anchor investor expectations around duration.

The views expressed herein do not constitute research, investment advice or trade recommendations, and do not necessarily represent the views of all AB portfolio-management teams and are subject to change over time.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Shopify: Why Holding The Line Still Makes Sense Amid Market Uncertainty
data.description:
The last time I discussed Shopify (NASDAQ:SHOP)(TSX:SHOP:CA) I gave it a hold rating on January 19, 2025, due to valuation concerns. I was correct that caution was warranted, as the company could not live up to the market's high expectations going into its fourth-quarter earnings report. Although it had a strong holiday shopping season and reported excellent fourth-quarter 2024 revenue growth, the market was disappointed in its mid-teen first-quarter 2025 free cash flow ("FCF") guidance. Since my hold call, the stock has been down nearly 19%, compared to the S&P 500's (SPX) 10% decline as of April 14, 2025. However, despite the negative sentiment toward the stock, long-term investors still have good reasons to hold it, including increasing success in its transition upmarket, steady revenue growth, and improving profitability.

On the earnings call, Shopify's Chief Financial Officer ("CFO") noted that the fourth quarter was the company's seventh consecutive quarter of consistent pro forma revenue growth of 25% or more, a solid indicator of demand for the company's services and execution of its business strategy. Gross Merchandise Value (GMV), the total dollar value of all merchandise sold by merchants through the Shopify platform, has surpassed 20% for six straight quarters.

Despite the market's near-term disappointment with the first quarter 2025 FCF forecast, Shopify has had excellent FCF trends over the past two years. It has achieved positive FCF for the last nine quarters and has produced six straight quarters of double-digit free cash flow margins. If the company remains on track, these revenue growth and FCF trends signal excellent long-term revenue growth potential.

This article discusses how Shopify has broadened its target market from small and medium-sized businesses ("SMBs") to large enterprise-level retailers. It will also discuss the company's fourth quarter 2024 earnings report, risks, valuation, and why the stock remains a hold.

Shopify moving up the value chain

Since the company's beginning, Shopify has mainly supported SMBs. However, the company has reached a size that if it wants to maintain a solid revenue growth rate above 20%, it needs to win the business of more prominent enterprise merchants.

The company ended 2024 by generating $8.9 billion in annual revenue. Shopify's base revenue has grown large enough that the law of large numbers may start coming into play. In Shopify's case, the law of large numbers means that at its current revenue base, maintaining high growth rates will become more challenging as the company grows revenue. For instance, achieving a 20% growth on $1 billion in revenue is far easier than achieving the same percentage growth on $10 billion in revenue. As a result, it can no longer rely strictly on an SMB customer base for growth. The average revenue per SMB customer is often lower than that of a large enterprise. To significantly increase overall revenue growth as its base expands, Shopify needs to win larger deals with enterprise merchants to gain more bang for the buck each time it wins a new account. Large enterprise merchants often produce higher GMV than SMBs, utilize more of Shopify's services, and pay higher subscription fees (especially for Shopify Plus).

The company uses a land-and-expand strategy, giving potential clients options for starting with Shopify. Company President Harley Finkelstein said on the fourth quarter of 2024 earnings call:

I think one of the great things we did was we created a bunch of options for them [enterprise clients]. If they want headless, we have Hydrogen. If they want, one size fits all, we have Plus. If they want something that's specific, specific component, we have CCS [Commerce Components by Shopify].

Headless refers to separating an e-commerce setup's front end (the user interface, the website, mobile app, or other ways consumers interact with the company) from its back end (core functionality, such as product management, order processing, and payments). Shopify's bread and butter is the back end. Suppose a client wants to use Shopify's back-end software but wants to use a third party to build its front end (make a mobile app or design its e-commerce website); customers can do so. Hydrogen is its User Interface ("UI") technology. If customers want to create a UI using Shopify's technology but use a different company's back-end technology, they can do so with Hydrogen. Plus refers to the Shopify Plus platform, an enterprise-level e-commerce platform. The company's strategy with CCS is to sell modular components with various vital e-commerce and omnichannel functionalities to retailers to get its foot in the door. Eventually, it upsells these new clients to the full Shopify suite.

The company started its efforts to attract larger retailers around two years ago and is just now beginning to see its efforts pay off. Shopify President Harley Finkelstein recently attended the January 2025 National Retail Federation (NRF) event, where he found evidence that the company's strategies to move upmarket are gaining traction. Finkelstein said on the company's fourth-quarter earnings call in February:

We finished NRF in January. Pretty much every retailer and every major brand on the planet is now looking to Shopify, or at least, coming and introducing themselves to Shopify to figure out how, if we can work with them. That was not happening even 12 months ago. And I think the two things that really are helpful to them is one, the product. This is a future-proof product, which means no matter where they want to sell, long-term, they can. But also the value, Shopify is incredibly high value. Relative to all the other enterprise players, it's still the best deal in town..

On the earnings call, he provided concrete examples of enterprise-level adoption, including Westwing, BarkBox, Reebok, David's Bridal, Hunter Douglas, Warner Music Group (WMG), and Crocs (CROX). Some of the above companies have historically avoided doing business with Shopify because they used systems built by their in-house engineering teams or third-party legacy commerce software companies. These wins signal a change in large retailers' perception of Shopify.

Another reason enterprises have become attracted to Shopify's solutions is that the switching costs to use Shopify are declining. One big obstacle to switching to new platforms or software is the lengthy change period, which can disrupt ongoing operations. Finkelstein clarified on the earnings call that customers can implement Shopify's solutions relatively quickly. Shopify's President said, "Some of the brands that launched in the past quarter, launch at this record speed where it doesn't take 12 months to 18 months to launch, where it takes three months to launch, it's becoming incredibly compelling."

Investors should monitor Shopify's ability to win large enterprise customers. Suppose the company maintains its momentum in winning these types of customers. In that case, it should maintain solid double-digit revenue growth over the next decade, a significant reason to remain invested in this company.

Company fundamentals

The company's fourth-quarter GMV increased 25.7% to $94.5 billion. Chief Financial Officer ("CFO") Jeff Hoffmeister said on the earnings call that the figure was the "highest quarterly GMV rate since the pandemic-driven growth rate of 31% in Q4 2021." He also said, "The Q4 GMV results were driven primarily by same-store sales growth from our existing merchants, led by our Plus merchants, growth in the number of merchants on our platform, continued strength internationally with GMV outside North America growing 33% in Q4." In addition to Shopify's climb up the value chain, investors should also pay attention to international growth, as eventually, the U.S. and Canadian markets will become saturated, and the company will become more reliant on growth outside the North American region. The company's EMEA (Europe, the Middle East, and Africa) region GMV increased 37% year over year.

Its 2024 annual GMV increased 23.9% to $292.3 billion. Although increased GMV doesn't directly translate into increased revenue because the company doesn't take a percentage cut of GMV, more activity on the platform indirectly boosts income. Most GMV comes from Shopify Plus merchants, larger businesses that pay significantly higher subscription fees than those on Basic or Shopify plans. As Shopify Plus merchants increase sales, they will likely remain on the platform and purchase other services.

Shopify's fourth-quarter monthly recurring revenue (MRR), the total monthly revenue a company expects to generate from its recurring subscriptions, increased by 23.6% to $178 million. Shopify Plus comprised 33% of MRR for the quarter, flat sequentially. Its fourth-quarter 2024 MRR growth rate is healthy for a company of Shopify's size. Since recurring revenue is more stable and predictable than one-time sales, investors will often value companies with higher recurring revenue growth rates more than those without. Investors should remain aware that Shopify incentivizes smaller customers to try the platform through a paid trial, generally one month in length. However, in the fourth quarter of 2024, it shifted to a three-month paid trial in some markets, which pressured its fourth-quarter MRR. The company expects this new paid trial period to impact the first and second quarters' MRR. The CFO gave the reason for the shift on the earnings call:

For nearly every lens of our business like geography or merchant type, the three month trial yielded better long-term results for merchants and Shopify. Therefore, we are moving to three month trials in most geographies. As a reminder, paid trials are just one of our Merchant acquisition tools.

Shopify's fourth-quarter 2024 revenue increased 31% year over year to $2.8 billion, beating the company's forecast for mid-to-high-twenties revenue growth and analysts' estimates by $83.68 million. The company's full-year 2024 revenue increased 26% to $8.9 billion. CFO Hoffmeister said the following on the earnings call:

The revenue outperformance relative to our outlook was driven by North America representing a larger percentage of GMV than expected. More of our products are available in North America versus other geographies, so outperformance in the U.S. and Canada brings higher revenue per merchant.

The company reports revenue in two segments: Merchants Solutions and Subscription Solutions. The company defines Merchants Solutions in its 2024 10-K:

We offer a variety of merchant solutions to augment those provided through our subscriptions and to address the broad array of functionality merchants commonly require, including accepting payments, securing working capital and shipping...We principally generate merchant solutions revenues from payment processing fees and currency conversion fees from Shopify Payments, our fully integrated payment processing service that allows our merchants to accept and process payment cards online and offline. We also generate merchant solutions revenue from other product offerings including our lending and financial products (to merchants in eligible geographies), referral fees from partners and the sale of shipping labels.

Shopify's fourth-quarter Merchants Solutions revenue increased 32.6% year over year to $2.15 billion. The CFO said the segment's revenue growth was "primarily driven by the continued strength in GMV and increased penetration of Shopify payments." Shopify Payments, the platform's integrated payment processor, processed 35% more of the platform's GMV during the quarter to reach $61 billion, or 64% of total GMV, up four points from the fourth quarter of 2023.

Gross Payments Volume (GPV) is the total dollar value of all the merchandise sold by merchants through the Shopify platform that was processed using Shopify Payments. The $61 billion in the previous paragraph is Shopify Payments' GPV during the fourth quarter. CFO Hoffmeister highlighted the reasons for a rising GPV on the earnings call:

Several factors drove the quarter's higher gross payments volume, including the strong performance of merchants, who use Shopify payments, an increasing percentage of which are on Shopify Plus, more merchants across the globe adopting payments, and greater penetration of Shop Pay, which was 41% of GPV in the quarter. As a reminder, Q4 is a quarter which traditionally sees the highest percentage of revenue from payments. For the year GPV penetration was 62%, up from 58% in 2023.

Some people confuse Shopify Payments (a payment processor) with Shop Pay, a one-click checkout feature that speeds up purchasing by saving a consumer's shipping and billing information. When consumers use Shop Pay on a website, they don't have to repeat filling out shipping and payment information every time they make a new purchase.

Shopify defines Subscription Solutions in its 2024 10-K:

We generate subscription solutions revenues primarily through the sale of subscriptions to our platform and from variable platform fees, as well as through the sale of subscriptions to our Point of Sale Pro ("POS Pro") offering, the sale of apps, the registration of domain names and the sale of themes. We offer pricing plans designed to meet the needs of our current and prospective merchants. While most merchants subscribe to our Basic and Shopify plans, the majority of our gross merchandise volume ("GMV") has been generated from merchants subscribing to our Shopify Plus plan and enterprise offerings.

Shopify's fourth-quarter 2024 Subscription Solutions revenue increased 27% over the fourth quarter of last year to $666 million. Shopify's CFO said, "The growth was driven by an increase in the number of merchants on our platform and, to a lesser degree, higher variable platform fees and the benefit from the Plus pricing change."

The company's gross profit increased 27.3% year over year to $1.35 billion for the fourth quarter and 27.2% to $4.5 billion for the full year 2024. Its fourth quarter 2024 gross margin decreased 140 basis points ("bps") to 48.1%, and its annual gross margin expanded 60 bps to 50.4%.

The company's fourth-quarter Subscription Solutions gross margin decreased 160 bps year over year to 79.9%. Hoffmeister said on the earnings call, "The decrease was mainly due to higher cloud and infrastructure hosting costs, in part to support our higher volume fourth quarter, but we do not expect this to have as much of an impact going forward."

Its fourth-quarter 2024 Merchant Solutions gross margin decreased by 100 bps year over year to 38.2%. The CFO said the following about the Merchant Solutions gross margin decline:

The decrease was primarily driven by lower non-cash revenues from certain partnerships which carry a high gross margin and the impact from the expanded partnership with PayPal. Partially offsetting these headwinds in Q4 was strength in our other Merchant Solutions products led by international selling previously known as markets.

In short, the company made less from higher gross revenue sources than in previous periods, and possibly more customers used PayPal (PYPL) during the quarter under newly negotiated terms that were less favorable to Shopify's gross margin. The product that involves international selling, referred to as "previously known as Markets," is the cross-border e-commerce technology collaboration with Global-E Online (GLBE).

Shopify's ability to control operating expense ("OpEx") growth to revenue growth over the last two years has driven the company's outsized increase in operating profitability over the previous two years. For example, the company reduced its fourth-quarter 2022 OpEx (excluding the real estate charge) as a percentage of revenue from 52% to 36% in the fourth quarter of 2023 to 32% in the fourth quarter of 2024. Its 2024 annual OpEx as a percentage of revenue was 38%, the lowest in company history.

The company also increased research and development (R&D), sales and marketing (S&M), and general and administrative (G&A) expenses at a slower rate than its revenue during the fourth quarter, increasing its operating leverage. For a company with operating leverage, accelerating revenue growth will result in profits growing faster than revenue. Conversely, the opposite also occurs: decelerating revenue shrinks profits faster than revenue. Shopify has increased its quarterly year-over-year revenue growth since it bottomed at 16% in the second quarter of 2022, and operating leverage has become a tailwind for profitability.

Management attributes most of the decrease in R&D, S&M, and G&A expenses relative to revenue to a headcount reduction. In other words, the company has become more efficient and is doing more with fewer people. At the end of 2022, the company had around 11,600 employees. Shopify's CFO said on the earnings call, "As of December 31st, 2024, our headcount was just under 8,100 employees, down from approximately 8,300 at the end of 2023."

CFO Jeff Hoffmeister said the following about the company's operating margin on the earnings call:

Operating margin for the year hit 12%, double that of our previous peak in 2021, and operating margin for the quarter was 17%. All of these metrics represent the strongest profitability levels that we have achieved since going public in 2015, all while maintaining and in many instances accelerating growth at scale.

Its fourth-quarter 2024 stock-based compensation ("SBC") was $118 million, or around 4.1% of revenue, which is excellent for its peer group.

Company Name SBC-to-quarterly revenue
Amazon (AMZN) 2.66%
Shopify 4.10%
Block (XYZ) 5.25%
BigCommerce (BIGC) 7.84%
Adobe (ADBE) 8.31%
Wix (WIX) 13.42%

Shopify's fourth-quarter 2024 diluted earnings-per-share ("EPS") increased by 94% to $0.99, beating analysts' estimates by $0.66.

Shopify's trailing 12-month ("TTM") cash flow from operations ("CFO") to sales is 18.20%, nearly the same as it was during the peak of pandemic-fueled growth in 2021.

Shopify's fourth quarter 2024 CFO is $615 million, and TTM CFO is $1.61 billion.

Shopify's fourth quarter 2024 free cash flow ("FCF") was $611 million, and the FCF margin was 21.7%, slightly above company forecasts of 21%. Management attributed the better-than-expected FCF margin to "revenue outperformance."

Shopify's annual 2024 FCF increased 77% to $1.59 billion; the annual 2024 FCF margin was 18%. Despite the solid FCF margin growth over the last two years, some investors were disappointed to hear on the earnings call that management is more interested in driving revenue growth than driving higher FCF margins. CFO Hoffmeister said the following (emphasis added):

To be clear, we are a growth company. We plan to prioritize investing further in key areas like our core platform, international, B2B enterprise and offline, as opposed to driving for higher free cash flow margins in the near-term. It's simply the right thing to do with the immense opportunities we see ahead, but delivers a profitability level that we are proud of and believe we can maintain without impacting future growth.

The company ended 2024 with $5.48 billion in cash and short-term investments. It has no long-term debt.

First Quarter 2025 guidance

Before Shopify reported fourth-quarter earnings, analysts estimated revenue of $2.32 billion, which translates to 24.66% year-over-year revenue growth, within the "mid-twenty percentage rate" forecast. After earnings, analysts raised the estimated first quarter 2025 revenue to $2.33 billion, a revenue growth rate of 25.20%.

The first quarter 2025 guidance of a mid-teens FCF margin implies a 200- to 400-basis-point expansion over the first quarter of 2024. Still, considering the strength of Shopify's fourth-quarter results, the market may have expected even higher first-quarter FCF margin guidance. It also may have wanted to see the company issue full-year FCF margin guidance, which management did not provide. The first question from an analyst on the earnings call attempted to gain an answer about what the company expects its FCF margin to be for 2025. Chief Financial Officer Hoffmeister avoided directly commenting on whether the FCF margin will increase sequentially at the same pace as the first quarter throughout the year. Instead, he said:

In terms of the overall level of free cash flow margins, how we think about it and we believe we've gotten to a very good level which allows us to obviously maintain this profitability, but also invest back into the future and what we want to accomplish. So I made some comments in the past as it relates to the quarterly evolution of free cash flows, I stand by that. As you know, Q4 traditionally is just our -- just in terms of GMV dollars is our largest quarter in terms of the GMV, which then translates into revenue which obviously then translates down into free cash flow margin. But in the aggregate, in terms of how we think about these margins, again I go back to my perspective on this feels like we're at the right level.

In other words, management believes a mid-teen FCF margin provides healthy profitability while leaving room for the company to invest in market share and revenue growth initiatives, implying a mid-teens level is a targeted level and not to expect much more FCF margin expansion this year. The market is disappointed with the implication that Shopify could exit 2025 with a mid-teens FCF margin. Analysts and investors wanted more. However, although possibly not seeing a higher FCF margin in 2025 might be a near-term disappointment, investing in business expansion may be a better long-term decision, leading to more sustainable revenue growth.

Risks

Shopify has another incentive for moving upmarket besides the law of large numbers. Some people believe Shopify's core SMB customer base has a high churn rate, especially in poor economic environments, as smaller businesses will cut back on spending faster and fail more often than larger businesses in tough economic times. So, its revenue will usually decline faster in a recession than that of a company like Amazon. Shopify doesn't explicitly publish churn or customer retention rates regularly with each earnings report. Investors may infer these rates through the company's MRR, merchant growth, and sometimes management commentary on customer cohorts. Still, some are skittish about investing in Shopify because it can be challenging to determine how quickly the churn rate rises. Some investors expect the economy to do poorly in 2025 and are rightly concerned about how Shopify will perform if inflation returns or the economy enters a recession.

When the U.S. Index of Consumer Sentiment goes below 50, it generally means bad things for the SMB retailers that still make up a significant chunk of Shopify's customer base. On April 11, the news media reported that the University of Michigan consumer survey declined from 57 to 50.8. Suppose SMBs become more conservative in spending or business failures rise; Shopify could miss its revenue forecasts, and investors may revalue the stock downwards.

So, Shopify management also wants larger customers to ensure better revenue stability when the economy becomes more unstable in the future. However, winning over larger customers is easier said than done. Several more prominent companies with more financial resources and possibly better name recognition, such as Amazon, Adobe, and Salesforce (CRM), have large customer bases that may include some of the companies that Shopify wants to gain as clients. Most larger software and cloud companies have a significant switching cost moat. Unless Shopify presents larger enterprise customers with a better solution than its larger peers already provide, it may have difficulty prying away customers.

Valuation

Shopify's price-to-sales (P/S) ratio is 12.29, below its five-, seven-, and ten-year medians but slightly above its three-year median. Suppose the stock traded at its seven-year median of 17.54; its stock price would be $119.97, a 43% rise over the April 16 closing price of $83.96. If it sold at its three-year median, the stock price would be $81.19.

When I made a peer comparison based on one-year forward P/S ratio-to-estimated revenue growth in my article on ServiceNow, I stated:

Generally, the determining factors over what separates a higher valued company based on this metric from a lower valued one are FCF margin first (the market likes companies that can convert revenue into FCF the best), secondly, profitability (operating margin in this case), and lastly, revenue growth. Occasionally, there are outlier companies that differentiate themselves based on some intangible, like the strength of brand, revenue reliability, customer loyalty, pricing power, company reputation, quality of the management team, and intellectual property, or other sources of a moat (cost leadership, switching cost, network effect).

The same statement applies to the following comparison of Shopify to its peers.

Company Name One-year forward P/S ratio One-year forward estimated revenue growth One-year Forward P/S ratio-to-estimated revenue growth TTM FCF margin TTM Operating Margin One-year forward EPS growth estimates
Shopify 8.27 20.69% 0.40 18% 12.11% 27.18%
Amazon 2.49 10.03% 0.25 1.9% 10.75% 19.55%
Adobe 5.80 9.69% 0.60 41.7% 36.29% 12.02%
BigCommerce 1.12 5.37% 0.21 1.5% -8.01% 37.62%
Wix 4.00 13.05% 0.30 27.1% 5.69% 19.49%
Block 1.18 10.38% 0.11 6.3% 6.99% 19.05%

BigCommerce and Block have the lowest valuations in this comparison because of their single-digit or negative operating and FCF margins. Adobe has the highest one-year forward P/S ratio-to-estimated revenue growth because it has the highest operating and FCF margins, despite Shopify growing revenue much faster. For the market to value Shopify similarly to Adobe, it must improve its FCF margin. Generally, a healthy FCF margin for a software company is around 20% to 25%. If Shopify had an FCF margin in the high 20s to low 30s at its current expected revenue growth rate today, the market would likely value it above Adobe. However, Shopify's FCF margin may remain at 18% for the year, and investors should not expect the stock price to gain much traction soon.

Shopify's TTM price-to-earnings (P/E) ratio is 54.14, well above its Information Technology ("IT") sector median of 25.07. Therefore, some might conclude that the market overvalues the stock.

Shopify's one-year forward P/E to growth ("PEG") ratio is 1.63 (Shopify's 2026 forward P/E of 44.44 divided by analysts' estimated EPS growth rate of 27.18%). Typically, investors will allow a growth investment to reach a PEG ratio of 2.0 before considering it overvalued. Suppose it sold at a PEG ratio of 2.0 today; the stock price would be $102.74, a 26.5% rise over the April 16 closing price. In a theoretical economic climate of low inflation, low interest rates, and a GDP growth rate of 3%, investors may have pushed a stock of Shopify's quality much higher than a 2.0 PEG ratio. However, with Federal Reserve Chairman Jerome Powell giving a downbeat assessment of the economy and investors increasingly fearing inflation and recession, the market may be reluctant to push the valuation much higher than it is today.

Typically, I would perform a reverse discount cash flow analysis or other FCF-based valuation. However, I am now reluctant to do so with Shopify because it doesn't have a long enough history of consistent, positive FCF growth, making any such assessment unreliable.

Shopify remains a hold

Although I consider Shopify undervalued in the long term based on its PEG ratio, the market will likely not favor it until the danger of stagflation or a recession recedes. The odds are that investors will be able to buy this stock at a better valuation over the next year. People who have not bought it yet should hold off until economic uncertainty clears up. I would wait until the Michigan Consumer Survey ends its decline and begins recovering before considering buying. As long as consumer sentiment worsens, the market will likely be unable to get excited about companies in the retailing industry, and Shopify's valuation will likely continue to decline in the near term.

However, this company has the financial strength to survive a recession, and its investments in its platform, international growth, B2B enterprise, and omnichannel should pay off. The company's annual 2024 revenue of $8.9 billion is only around 1% of its 2023 total addressable market ("TAM") of $849 billion, with the company expecting its TAM to expand further. Shopify has plenty of revenue growth runway left, and if it successfully raises its FCF margin over time into the 20%-25% range, it will likely produce market-beating returns over the next ten years. I rate the stock a hold rating for long-term investors.

data.title: CEF Weekly Review: Are We In Another CEF Deleveraging Wave?
data.description:
Welcome to another installment of our CEF Market Weekly Review, where we discuss closed-end fund ("CEF") market activity from both the bottom-up – highlighting individual fund news and events – as well as the top-down – providing an overview of the broader market. We also try to provide some historical context as well as the relevant themes that look to be driving markets or that investors ought to be mindful of.

This update covers the period through the second week of April. Be sure to check out our other weekly updates covering the business development company ("BDC") as well as the preferreds/baby bond markets for perspectives across the broader income space.

Market Action

CEF sector returns were mixed on the week with Muni sectors underperforming and equity-linked sectors finishing in the green - the opposite of what we saw the previous week. The vast majority of sectors saw a drop in NAVs, primarily due to the rise in Treasury yields while all but one sector discount tightened.

Year-to-date, sector performance is mixed. Munis are still hanging on to mild outperformance while equity-linked sectors are deeper in the red.

April is shaping up to be the worst month for CEFs since late 2022.

The whipsaws in the CEF market are the largest in the post-COVID environment.

Discounts have recovered somewhat from the recent plunge but remain on the wider side of the recent range. However, what's interesting is that the market has generally weathered the situation relatively well. Discounts are not nearly as wide as they were in 2023 and the size of the sell-off has, so far, been relatively modest.

Market Themes

There was a question on the service about CEF deleveraging and whether that’s affecting a lot of funds at present. The short answer is no. If we take the highest volatility CEF sector - CLO Equity - NAVs here have fallen at most around 10-15% since the start of March (ECC showed a 10% drop in March with other sector CEFs at lower levels).

If we start off with a relatively high leverage level of 40%, the sell-off, if evaluated conservatively, only gets us to a bit below 45%. If we assume roughly half of the liabilities in debt and half in preferreds that gets us to a bond asset coverage of 3.9x (well above the 3x level) and preferreds asset coverage of 2.275x (more than 10% above the 2x level).

The NAV moves in other sectors are much more benign - the loan CEF sector NAV drop is around 5% which isn’t really going to make much of a difference on leverage. Realistically, we would need about the same kind of move in spreads from early March levels to the April peak for deleveraging to be a serious threat to CEFs.

As discussed a number of times, it’s generally best to avoid funds with strict CEF leverage caps (e.g. Nuveen and John Hancock preferreds CEFs, among others) as these funds may act preemptively even before the caps are hit. This would cause them to sell assets and only buy them back at higher prices, locking in losses for investors in a typically mean-reverting credit market.

Market Commentary

CLO Equity CEF NAVs are starting to come in. EIC had a 5% drop in March while ECC had a 10% drop with other sector funds in the middle of this range. That was on the back of a 0.65% widening in HY corporate bond spreads in March. Spreads are wider by around 0.8% so far in April, which could drive a similar drop in the sector if spreads remain stable. This means that the sector is not particularly cheap on a discount basis (with most funds likely trading at small premiums based on today's NAV) but decently valued on an underlying basis. When you have this discrepancy, discounts matter a bit less for allocation purposes, as the potential upward move in NAVs alongside higher yields are more important for total returns than discounts.

RiverNorth Capital and Income Fund (RSF) completed its quarterly repurchase offer. The fund has been buying back shares for a long while with a relatively generous offer of 5% share repurchase at the NAV. The latest pro-ration factor came in at 11% which is pretty good. The discount has been trading around 6-8% which offers a 2-2.5% annualized alpha on top of the historic performance. The fund is a nice diversifier to the typical CEF with a mostly small business whole loan portfolio. Ever since it redeemed its preferred, it has been trading with no leverage, making it a relatively low beta asset.

Stance and Takeaways

This week, we took advantage of both sharply wider credit spreads and discounts to top up our CEF allocation. Recall that we significantly took down our CEF exposure over the last 18 months on the back of expensive credit valuations, tight discounts and a low level of margin yield on leveraged assets. We added two CEFs - the Ares Dynamic Credit Allocation Fund (ARDC) and the PIMCO High Income Fund (PHK). The two are both multi-sector credit funds with strong performance and an attractive valuation.

Editor's Note: This article covers one or more microcap stocks. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: McDonald's: A Great Company To Hold Long Term, But Currently Too Overvalued
data.description:
Today, I want to take a quick look at McDonald's (NYSE:MCD) and explain why I rate the company as a HOLD. In my eyes, MCD is doing a great job growing their net income and FCF; the issue is that through the past years they have become overvalued. To understand why my rating is as such, we will take a look at the most recent earnings report, to then analyze the company's fundamentals through the 9 Pillar Analysis and finally calculate their intrinsic value using 4 of our intrinsic valuation models.

Analysis

Q4'24 Earnings Report

On the 10th of February, MCD announced their Q4'24 results. Their Revenue fell short of the $6.45B consensus mark, reported at $6.39B. Regarding the Non-GAAP EPS, that was also a miss by $0.03, reported at $2.83 but expected at $2.86.

Although these misses do not seem that bad, something that is important to understand is that the company's US-based sales have seen the worst drop since the pandemic, dropping by 1.40% compared to the consensus mark of 0.41%. Even though the company's global sales grew by 0.40%, better than the expected decrease of 0.93%, as the US represents 41.01% of the company's overall revenue, the revenue for Q4 was lower than in Q3 as already mentioned.

Normally, as I am a long-term investor, this small quarterly decrease wouldn't bother me, but in this case, I sense something bigger. As inflation has been really high through the past few years, which I already mentioned in numerous of my other articles, consumers have been heavily cutting on their spending. This is affecting all B2C businesses as they see an overall fall in sales volumes. Most of all, this is affecting non-essential products and services such as restaurants, cafés, home renovation and product stores, etc. One of such companies is MCD, which, as customers cut on spending, registers a decline in sales.

Before moving to the 9 Pillar analysis, there is one more thing I want to mention, and that is that through the past few years, MCD has been increasing their prices, which has been one of the reasons for their growing profitability and so net income. To put this into perspective, since 2019, average store pricing has increased by around 40.00%. Of course, the franchise can't raise their prices forever, so the question remains: what will happen when they can't increase the price tags on their products anymore?

In my eyes, these are all issues that can be solved and certainly don't put the company at high risk. What I am trying to say is that they might, in the future, see lower growth than what is expected, especially with rising inflation and possible tariffs, which would force consumers to spend even less on unnecessary eating out. Therefore, the company's current valuation is, in my eyes, too high compared both to their current and future earnings potential.

9 Pillar Analysis

To understand what I mean, we will take a look at the 5Y PE and P/FCF, Pillars 1 and 2, but first, let me explain what the 9 pillars actually are.

Pillars 1 and 2 represent the 5-year Price-to-Earnings (P/E) and Price-to-Free Cash Flow (P/FCF) ratios, respectively. Pillars 3, 4, and 5 illustrate the growth in revenue, net income, and free cash flow over the past 5 years. Pillar 6 focuses on the 5-year trailing profitability, while Pillar 7 highlights the change in shares outstanding. Pillar 8 tracks dividend growth, and Pillar 9 calculates the ratio of long-term liabilities to 5-year free cash flow minus dividends, which assesses a company's capability to pay down debt within a reasonable period while still paying dividends. MCD gets a rating of 6/9, with the three pillars in the red being the valuation and debt levels.

Valuation (Pillars 1 and 2)

Looking at the 5Y PE ratio, we can see that it is higher than the average stock PE of around 22.50, sitting at 24.94. Personally, I wouldn't mind paying 25x earnings for the company, as they are a well-established brand that has steady financial and dividend CAGRs. The issue is that currently, the PE is sitting at 27.14, which in my eyes is too expensive even for a well-established company like MCD.

Above you can see that indeed, today, MCD is trading the most expensive since 2020, even though the future growth prospects aren't too great.

Regarding the P/FCF, that is currently sitting at 33.45, higher than the 5Y average of 27.29, and the highest it has been over the past 5 years. So by looking at these two values, we can see that indeed, MCD is currently overvalued compared to what they have been trading in the past.

Financial Growth (Pillars 3, 4 and 5)

The reason why I don't rate the company as a SELL is because of their great financial growth through the past 5 years, which if continues, to an extent supports the company's higher valuation.

The revenue has been growing at a 5Y CAGR of 6.47%, from $19.21B in 2020 to $25.92B in 2024.

Though this isn't as impressive as the net income, which has been growing at a CAGR of 15.11% over the past 5Y, from $4.73B in 2020, to $8.22B in 2024. My sentiment is further supported by the 10Y CAGR, which is sitting at 6.30% with the net income growing very steadily from $4.53B in 2015 to the current $8.22B.

This is the same case with the FCF, which has grown from $4.62B in 2020, to $6.67B in 2024, accounting for a CAGR of 11.01%. The 10Y CAGR is also sitting at a decent value of 4.10%. So overall, the growth is very decent for a stable company like MCD, but with inflation growing and customers becoming more wary of their spendings, the current valuation is in my eyes too bullish.

Debt (Pillar 9)

Now, before moving to the dividends, let's take a look at the third pillar in the red, and that is the debt. The company's LTL / 5Y FCF - Dividends ratio is sitting rather high at 6.71, much higher than the below 3.00 we would like to see. Essentially, this means that MCD would take around 34 years to pay off all their LTL while paying out their current dividend. Over the past 5 years, their LTL have actually increased by 1.44%, from $53.57B to $54.34B, which isn't great as it reaffirms the view that the company is heavily indebted and isn't doing much to change it.

Furthermore, their Current Ratio is sitting at 0.94, meaning that they have more current liabilities than current assets, again, exposing the company to danger from financial downturns.

Dividends (Pillar 8)

Looking at the dividends, what I want to point out is that the company's 5Y CAGR is sitting nice and high at 7.72%, with a decent dividend yield of 2.29%. The FCF payout ratio is also sitting at a decent range of 68.22%, leaving MCD with enough FCF to push into further dividend increases, paying down debt, buying back shares, etc. Though with their high debt, I would expect their CAGR to decrease into the future.

Intrinsic Value

Nevertheless, now that we have gone through the 9 Pillars, let's calculate the company's intrinsic value through our models.

For the Graham's Formula, we will plug in the 5Y EPS of $10.46, and based on the historical 5Y and 10Y net income CAGRs, predict the forward EPS CAGR at 9.33%. This will give us an intrinsic value of $199.24, 35.65% lower than the current trading price of $309.61.

For the second model, we will as always look to the DCF model, where I will plug in the 5Y average FCF of $6.23B, and based off of that, calculate the future FCF using a CAGR of 7.00%, based on the historical 5Y and 10Y CAGRs and the terminal rate of 2.50%. Finally, I will discount the FFCF at a rate of 7.33%, my desired rate of return, and get an intrinsic value of $173.34.

For the third valuation, we will compare the MCD's PE to the PE ratios of Starbucks and Restaurant Brands International to get an intrinsic value of $263.24.

And Finally, using the alternative DDM, I will project a dividend CAGR of 6.67%, slightly lower than the historical 5Y YOY growth as I do believe that the company will face some pressure from their LTL levels. Then, using the stock price next year predicted by analysts, I will calculate an intrinsic value of $356.01.

After averaging out the four values, we will arrive to an ultimate intrinsic value of $247.96, 19.61% lower than the current trading price of $308.44. With a margin of safety sitting at 10.00%, our acceptable buying price would be $222.93.

Conclusion

Concluding the analysis, based on McDonald's steady financial and dividend growth, along with a well-established customer base, I do have to rate them as a HOLD, even though they do have quite high debt and are overvalued compared to their historical multiples, and future growth. I trust that the company will grow into the future, but not at the rate that is currently priced in; therefore, again, I have to rate them as a HOLD.

data.title: VTI: A Fund That Carries More Risk In The Current Economic And Political Environment
data.description:
Investing strategies sometimes have to change, even as long-term objectives remain the same. While overreacting to short-term market cycles is usually not productive, most people still have to be willing to change their approach as different sectors perform better in constantly evolving economic and market conditions.

The most successful strategy for investing over most of the last decade has been to focus on the indexes. The S&P 500 (SPY) and Nasdaq have both offered investors impressive gains during this time period, and allocating capital to these benchmarks has been a simple and effective strategy.

One of the more well-known exchange-traded funds that seeks to similarly track the overall market is the Vanguard Total Stock Market ETF (NYSEARCA:VTI). VTI tracks the CRSP US Total Market Index. The fund's inception date was in May 2021 and this ETF reconstitutes quarterly.

VTI has offered investors total returns of 180.2 percent over the last decade, while the S&P 500 (SPY) has offered investors total returns of 197.3 percent during this same time period. While both VTI and the S&P 500 seek to track the broader market, the key difference between them is that the Vanguard fund includes smaller-cap equities.

Today, I am reiterating my sell rating for the Vanguard Total Stock Market ETF. This fund has more risk because of the investment's heavy exposure to big-cap tech and the current trade war between the US and China, and VTI's holdings in the technology sector are also not diversified. There are also signs the current economic slowdown is accelerating.

VTI has an expense ratio of .03 percent, 1.70 trillion in assets under management, and a current yield of 1.45 percent. The ETF's holdings are 31.60 percent technology, 14.01 percent the financials, 10.97 percent health care, 10.28 percent consumer cyclicals, 9.40 percent communication, 7.43 percent industrials, 6.28 percent consumer defensives, 3.23 percent energy, 2.80 percent utilities, 2.24 percent real estate, and 1.76 percent basic materials. VTI's largest holdings are Apple (AAPL), Microsoft (MSFT), NVIDIA (NVDA), and Amazon (AMZN). These 4 positions make up nearly 23 percent of the overall fund's assets.

The main reason VTI has performed well over the last 2 decades is because of the fund's significant big-cap tech holdings, but these assets carry more risk now. The tech industry gets nearly 60 percent of this sector's revenue from outside of the US, and most of the companies VTI has as core holdings, such as Apple, are heavily dependent on China, a country the US is currently in an increasingly hostile trade war.

This Vanguard fund has nearly 32 percent of the ETF's assets in the technology sector and several of this investment's core holdings, including Apple and Amazon, rely heavily on the world's second-largest economy for revenue. While Trump has temporarily exempted smartphones and computers from the tariffs he imposed on China, the Chinese government is still retaliating against US companies and these exceptions may not be permanent.

This Vanguard fund's lack of diversity makes the investment much more susceptible to the underperformance of the core holdings, which make up between 4-6 percent of the overall fund. VTI also has significant exposure to NVIDIA, which makes up nearly 6 percent of the overall fund, and this company has struggled over the last year as the chipmaker's growth rate has moderated with the company now maturing and also facing new competition in the cloud space and from startups.

There are also multiple indicators that the overall growth rate in the US and abroad is slowing. Recent retail spending numbers have come in soft, new auto sales were down significantly in February, and consumer debt remains at record levels. China also continues to deal with a housing crisis that is impacting consumer debt levels as well. While many large-cap tech companies should continue to perform well, VTI has significant exposure to a narrow group of companies that are heavily leveraged to the world's second-biggest economy.

All investment theses have risks, and Trump and President Xi of China could reach a resolution to these nations' trade concerns anytime soon. The Federal Reserve has also been hawkish on rates, but if Powell were to relent and begin to lower rates like his European counterparts, there would likely be a rally in equities and the dollar would likely fall against most major foreign currencies. VTI has significant international exposure since this fund primarily invests in large-cap companies.

VTI has performed reasonably well over the last decade, but this Vanguard's heavily concentrated positions in a narrow group of large-cap tech holdings should make returns more volatile moving forward, particularly in the current less predictable political and economic environment. While index investing has worked very well for some time, there are more diverse funds that still give investors exposure to big-cap tech and cyclical sectors such as the Capital Group Dividend Value ETF (CGDV) that should offer individuals a better risk profile moving forward.

data.title: NIE: This Equity/Convertible CEF Provides Tech Exposure With Reduced Downside Risk
data.description:
(Data below is sourced from the Virtus fund website unless otherwise stated.)

The Virtus Equity & Convertible Income Fund (NYSE:NIE) is a $660 million fund that invests at least 80% of the portfolio assets in a combination of equities and income producing convertible securities.

The equity portion varies from 40%- 80% of assets.

The convertible portion varies from 20%-60% of assets.

As of March 31, 2025, the fund had 57.5% invested in equities and 35.7% invested in convertibles with the rest in cash and a small amount of derivatives. No leverage is used.

The fund typically uses a covered call option strategy on about 70% of the stock positions. This allows the fund to earn additional income from selling the call option premiums and may help to reduce risk.

I last wrote about NIE about a year ago. The fund performed quite well throughout 2024 but has corrected significantly early in 2025 largely because of its large allocation to technology and the Mag 7 stocks.

Discount History

The fund is currently trading at a –10.01% discount to NAV which is in line with its 5 year average discount of -10.20%. But over the last year, the average discount was about –8.4%.

Here is the discount history from cefconnect:

Here is the NIE portfolio asset allocation as of March 31, 2025:

These allocations are dynamic and can change over time. But the managers generally keep the equity allocation very close to 60%.

Here is the sector allocation as of March 31, 2025. Information Technology and Financials were the highest two allocations, with close to a zero allocation to Energy stocks.
